jokes.text: Paralegal? I always thought that was a lawyer in a wheelchair.
jokes.setup: Michelle identifies herself as a paralegal
jokes.punchline: Hank's literal misinterpretation of the prefix 'para-'
jokes.explanation: Sharp wordplay that perfectly captures Hank's well-meaning but dim character. Economical setup/payoff with good originality in the literal interpretation.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Paralegal? I always thought that was a lawyer in a wheelchair.

jokes.text: That was very funny. I'm sure you hear that all the time. No, no. Not really.
jokes.setup: Michelle politely compliments Hank's paralegal joke
jokes.punchline: Hank's honest admission that he rarely gets laughs
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Hank's oblivious self-awareness. The deadpan delivery makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: That was very funny. I'm sure you hear that all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No, no. Not really.

jokes.text: Oh, no. No, no, no, no. I'm from Oklahoma. That's cow country. Someone who still eats red meat.
jokes.setup: Hank assumes she's vegetarian and suggests the Smokehouse
jokes.punchline: Her enthusiastic declaration of being from cow country
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from the contrast between Hollywood vegetarian assumptions and Midwest reality.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: Oh, no. No, no, no, no. I'm from Oklahoma. That's cow country. Someone who still eats red meat.

jokes.text: What are you talkin' about? I was kiddin'. You going to call her in? You bet your ass.
jokes.setup: Larry suggests calling the commercial actress as a joke
jokes.punchline: The crew member takes it completely seriously
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing the disconnect between Larry's sarcasm and the crew's literal mindset.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What are you talkin' about? I was kiddin'. You going to call her in?

dialogue.speaker: Unknown crew member
dialogue.line: You bet your ass.

jokes.text: 'Butthole' and 'surfing' shouldn't be put together. To me, someone's ass is gonna get waxed, and I don't think that's a good thing.
jokes.setup: Discussion of surf music and band name 'Butthole Surfers'
jokes.punchline: Larry's literal interpretation of 'ass getting waxed' in surfing context
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay connecting surfing terminology with the band name. Shows Larry's neurotic overthinking.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: 'Butthole' and 'surfing' shouldn't be put together. To me, someone's ass is gonna get waxed, and I don't think that's a good thing.

jokes.text: Bob Saget says that all the time. Really? All the time. That's good to know.
jokes.setup: Larry's comment about not wanting your ass waxed
jokes.punchline: Hank's random assertion that Bob Saget constantly says this
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Hank moment. The randomness of invoking Bob Saget and Michelle's polite acceptance is gold.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Bob Saget says that all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: All the time.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: That's good to know.

jokes.text: It's a big fish on, but the thing is Artie gave that to me. It's a whole fishing tackle box/wallet combination.
jokes.setup: Michelle compliments the fish on Hank's wallet
jokes.punchline: Revelation that it's an absurd tackle box/wallet hybrid from Artie
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag that establishes both Hank's earnestness and Artie's bizarre gift-giving.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It's a big fish on, but the thing is Artie gave that to me. It's a whole fishing tackle box/wallet combination.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, but the housekeeper's not here, so we'll have to just drink it out of the bottle. Straight out of the bottle? Straight.
jokes.setup: Michelle asks for more wine
jokes.punchline: Hank's casual admission that without his housekeeper, they must drink from the bottle
jokes.explanation: Reveals Hank's complete dependence on his housekeeper and social awkwardness in a relatable way.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, yeah, but the housekeeper's not here, so we'll have to just drink it out of the bottle.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: Straight out of the bottle?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Straight.

jokes.text: Chocolate is an aphrodisiac, and the mud part is all chocolate. Oh, my God. What? I think it's working.
jokes.setup: Hank explains chocolate is an aphrodisiac while feeding her mud pie
jokes.punchline: Michelle's immediate 'it's working' response
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/punchline with good timing. Michelle's immediate response sells the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Chocolate is an aphrodisiac, and the mud part is all chocolate.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: Oh, my God.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: I think it's working.

jokes.text: Sitter? Dog sitter? No, the baby-sitter. Um, I have a 6-year-old son.
jokes.setup: Michelle mentions calling the sitter
jokes.punchline: Hank's assumption it's a dog sitter, then reality check
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that reveals Hank's Hollywood bubble assumptions about single women.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: Sitter?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Dog sitter?

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: No, the baby-sitter. Um, I have a 6-year-old son.

jokes.text: Well, isn't that... isn't that just great?
jokes.setup: Learning Michelle has a child
jokes.punchline: Hank's obviously forced enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Subtle cringe moment where Hank's disappointment leaks through his fake enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, isn't that... isn't that just great?

jokes.text: quite frankly, Roger... is that... is that your husband? No, that's my son.
jokes.setup: Hank starting to make assumptions about Roger
jokes.punchline: His assumption that Roger is Michelle's husband rather than her son
jokes.explanation: Shows Hank's confusion and discomfort with the domestic situation

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: quite frankly, Roger... is that... is that your husband?

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: No, that's my son.

jokes.text: Roger... is that your husband? No, that's my son.
jokes.setup: Michelle mentions Roger doesn't like men she meets
jokes.punchline: Hank's confused assumption about who Roger is
jokes.explanation: Shows Hank not listening properly in his panic. Simple but effective character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: Roger... is that your husband?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No, that's my son.

jokes.text: Oh! I mean, kids are great. They have energy.
jokes.setup: Michelle says Roger will love meeting Hank
jokes.punchline: Hank's generic, unconvincing praise of children
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - trying to be supportive but clearly out of his depth with children.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh! I mean, kids are great. They have energy.

jokes.text: I don't know anyone who has an American car anymore. What do you mean? I drive an American car.
jokes.setup: Hank gushes about Michelle having an American car
jokes.punchline: Artie immediately contradicts him
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's obliviousness and tendency to romanticize.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I don't know anyone who has an American car anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I drive an American car.

jokes.text: It's a hell of a lot better than these rice burners and strudel wagons the rest of these assholes in Hollywood drive.
jokes.setup: Defending American cars
jokes.punchline: Artie's politically incorrect but colorful terminology
jokes.explanation: Pure Artie - the creative insults 'rice burners and strudel wagons' are memorable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: It's a hell of a lot better than these rice burners and strudel wagons the rest of these assholes in Hollywood drive.

jokes.text: Well, what about little Miguelito from Peru? You know, the kid that you adopted through Sally Struthers? All his letters pile up like goddamned junk mail.
jokes.setup: Hank claiming he can handle being a father figure
jokes.punchline: Artie reveals Hank's neglect of his sponsored child
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character assassination. The specificity (Sally Struthers, Peru) and the harsh reality check are perfectly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, what about little Miguelito from Peru? You know, the kid that you adopted through Sally Struthers? All his letters pile up like goddamned junk mail.

jokes.text: Well, what am I supposed to do? He wrote them in Spanish.
jokes.setup: Artie calling out Hank's neglect of Miguelito
jokes.punchline: Hank's pathetic excuse about the language barrier
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank logic - completely missing the point while finding a self-serving excuse. The absurdity is character-driven.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, what am I supposed to do? He wrote them in Spanish.

jokes.text: Showfolk belong with showfolk, Hank. If you're not careful, you're gonna mess this kid up and his mom.
jokes.setup: Hank's romance with 'real people'
jokes.punchline: Artie's cynical but probably accurate wisdom
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie - cynical Hollywood wisdom delivered with authority. The phrase 'showfolk belong with showfolk' is quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Showfolk belong with showfolk, Hank. If you're not careful, you're gonna mess this kid up and his mom.

jokes.text: Mama Lucia. Fabuloosa caboosa. Great ass, too.
jokes.setup: Attractive woman walking by
jokes.punchline: Artie's made-up Italian-sounding appreciation
jokes.explanation: Pure Artie character moment with his made-up expressions. 'Fabuloosa caboosa' is memorably ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Mama Lucia. Fabuloosa caboosa. Great ass, too.

jokes.text: Oh, that's because Phil doesn't have an office.
jokes.setup: Woman looking for Phil's office
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact revelation that Phil has no office
jokes.explanation: Efficient setup/punchline that establishes Phil's low status in the hierarchy.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Oh, that's because Phil doesn't have an office.

jokes.text: 20 bucks? I hope it's a day of beauty.
jokes.setup: Beverly asks for $20 for her niece's school raffle
jokes.punchline: Larry's assumption about what could cost that much
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about raffle ticket prices, delivered with Larry's neurotic specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: 20 bucks? I hope it's a day of beauty.

jokes.text: My money's missing. Gosh, Larry. If you don't wanna buy one, just say so. No, I believe you. My money's gone.
jokes.setup: Larry discovers missing money right after being asked for raffle money
jokes.punchline: Beverly's assumption he's making an excuse, his sincere clarification
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection creating awkward misunderstanding. Sets up the Lori Loughlin theft subplot.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: My money's missing.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Gosh, Larry. If you don't wanna buy one, just say so.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, I believe you. My money's gone.

jokes.text: It's more like a concept at this point.
jokes.setup: Asked about a sketch for an actress to read
jokes.punchline: Phil's vague admission there's no actual script
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hollywood double-speak. 'Concept at this point' is a beautifully vague non-answer.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: It's more like a concept at this point.

jokes.text: Oh, come on. Have a cookie. So there's nothing for me to read? You want something to read? We got papers here. We got everything.
jokes.setup: Actress asks for script, Keith offers cookies instead
jokes.punchline: Phil's desperate offer of random papers as reading material
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing the desperation of the fake meeting setup.

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: Oh, come on. Have a cookie.

dialogue.speaker: Actress
dialogue.line: So there's nothing for me to read?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: You want something to read? We got papers here. We got everything.

jokes.text: Listen, you gotta be very careful when you accuse a friend of stealing money because many years ago, I was in New York with Lenny Bruce, and, uh, he accused me of taking 50 bucks from him, and it turned very ugly and I popped him.
jokes.setup: Larry accusing Lori Loughlin of theft
jokes.punchline: Artie's story about punching Lenny Bruce over theft accusations
jokes.explanation: Great Artie moment with specific showbiz name-dropping. The casual mention of punching Lenny Bruce is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Listen, you gotta be very careful when you accuse a friend of stealing money because many years ago, I was in New York with Lenny Bruce, and, uh, he accused me of taking 50 bucks from him, and it turned very ugly and I popped him.

jokes.text: You know, you've told me this story before, and, uh, you took the money. That may be, but I'm just trying to warn you.
jokes.setup: Artie's cautionary Lenny Bruce story
jokes.punchline: Larry's matter-of-fact revelation that Artie was actually the thief
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of Artie's story. The casual delivery and Artie's non-denial make this exceptional character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know, you've told me this story before, and, uh, you took the money.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That may be, but I'm just trying to warn you.

jokes.text: I know we're early, it's just they were so speedy over there at Midas Muffler.
jokes.setup: Michelle and Roger arriving early
jokes.punchline: Her mention of the unglamorous car service location
jokes.explanation: Nice class contrast - Michelle's real-world concerns versus Hollywood glamour. Midas Muffler detail grounds the character.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: I know we're early, it's just they were so speedy over there at Midas Muffler.

jokes.text: Uh-oh, you must be Susan. You must be Roger. How you doin', champ? Fine. They took my mom's car and lifted it way high.
jokes.setup: Hank meeting Roger for the first time
jokes.punchline: Roger's innocent description of the car being lifted at Midas
jokes.explanation: Sweet moment showing a child's perspective on mundane adult activities. Sets up Roger's character.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Uh-oh, you must be Susan. You must be Roger. How you doin', champ?

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: Fine. They took my mom's car and lifted it way high.

jokes.text: Sacramento... that's the capital of California. Wow. You are correct. You must... you must be very smart.
jokes.setup: Hank mentions Sacramento
jokes.punchline: Roger's eager demonstration and Hank's patronizing response
jokes.explanation: Sets up Roger's geography skills while showing Hank's awkward interaction with children.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Sacramento... that's the capital of California. Wow. You are correct.

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: You must... you must be very smart.

jokes.text: South Dakota. Pierre. And what's the capital of North Dakota... Pepé Le Pew?
jokes.setup: Geography quiz continues with South Dakota/Pierre
jokes.punchline: Hank's attempt at humor with cartoon character reference
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank trying to connect with a kid through dated cartoon references. Shows his generational disconnect.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: South Dakota. Pierre. And what's the capital of North Dakota... Pepé Le Pew?

jokes.text: You're funny, Mr. Kingsley.
jokes.setup: Hank's Pepé Le Pew joke
jokes.punchline: Roger's polite but underwhelmed response
jokes.explanation: Child's diplomatic response to unfunny adult humor. The politeness makes it funnier than a direct rejection.

dialogue.speaker: Roger
dialogue.line: You're funny, Mr. Kingsley.

jokes.text: Those are beautiful earrings. Oh, thanks. I got 'em today. Oh, really? Yeah. I used the money I got from, uh... my wallet?
jokes.setup: Larry compliments Lori's earrings
jokes.punchline: Her casual mention of using money 'from' his wallet
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup for the confrontation. Lori's near-admission creates perfect awkward tension.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Those are beautiful earrings.

dialogue.speaker: Lori
dialogue.line: Oh, thanks. I got 'em today.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, really?

dialogue.speaker: Lori
dialogue.line: Yeah. I used the money I got from, uh... my wallet?

jokes.text: You know what? I always give them a couple hundred bucks because you know what? You know, they know me.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to explain away his missing money
jokes.punchline: His absurd claim of tipping valets hundreds of dollars for recognition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry neurosis - backing down from confrontation with an increasingly absurd cover story.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know what? I always give them a couple hundred bucks because you know what? You know, they know me.

jokes.text: That bird was supposed to shit on Larry's hand.
jokes.setup: Hank defending Roger vs. the mynah bird that shit on Larry
jokes.punchline: Artie's casual revelation that the bird shitting was intentional
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy reveal. The casual delivery of this cruel show business reality is perfect Artie.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That bird was supposed to shit on Larry's hand.

jokes.text: Come on. Reba McEntire doesn't need to do 2 songs. Some guy left her, and she's broke. Big fucking deal. We got it the first time.
jokes.setup: Discussion of show booking
jokes.punchline: Artie's dismissive summary of country music tropes
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie cynicism about music industry formulas. The reduction of country music to one story is sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Come on. Reba McEntire doesn't need to do 2 songs. Some guy left her, and she's broke. Big fucking deal. We got it the first time.

jokes.text: You don't see me setting up fake meetings just to meet Ed Asner.
jokes.setup: Calling out Phil's fake actress meetings
jokes.punchline: Beverly's random choice of Ed Asner as her fantasy meeting
jokes.explanation: Perfectly unexpected choice. Ed Asner as Beverly's crush is absurd and memorable character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: You don't see me setting up fake meetings just to meet Ed Asner.

jokes.text: Beverly, kiss my ass. You show it bare.
jokes.setup: Phil tells Beverly to kiss his ass
jokes.punchline: Beverly's comeback about showing it bare
jokes.explanation: Quick wordplay comeback. Not groundbreaking but economical and in character for Beverly.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Beverly, kiss my ass.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: You show it bare.

jokes.text: They're not horses, Phil.
jokes.setup: Phil calling actresses 'skittish'
jokes.punchline: Beverly's literal correction
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan correction of Phil's objectifying language. Beverly's literal mindedness works well.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: They're not horses, Phil.

jokes.text: This sort of thing happens in the movies, maybe, but we had no idea this sort of behavior was being conducted on the very premises of the Larry Sanders show.
jokes.setup: Agent confronting them about fake meetings
jokes.punchline: Beverly's over-formal, mock-innocent response
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Beverly's ability to play innocent while knowing exactly what's happening.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: This sort of thing happens in the movies, maybe, but we had no idea this sort of behavior was being conducted on the very premises of the Larry Sanders show.

jokes.text: Shut the fuck up, Phil!
jokes.setup: Beverly defending the show's reputation
jokes.punchline: Her sudden, harsh order to Phil
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Beverly can be both diplomatic and brutal. The sudden shift is effective.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Shut the fuck up, Phil!

jokes.text: You know what's odd is I wear a 2-piece.
jokes.setup: Discussion of auditioning in bathing suits
jokes.punchline: Her matter-of-fact revelation about her commercial audition attire
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor about the actress industry's objectification, delivered with unsettling casualness.

dialogue.speaker: Actress
dialogue.line: You know what's odd is I wear a 2-piece.

jokes.text: Something seems... Reba... she was anxious to get on the road. There was a big turquoise and silver fair in Santa Fe tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Larry questioning why Reba didn't do two songs
jokes.punchline: Artie's specific, absurd excuse about a craft fair
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific absurdist excuse. The detail of 'turquoise and silver fair' is perfectly Artie's improvisational BS.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Something seems... Reba... she was anxious to get on the road. There was a big turquoise and silver fair in Santa Fe tomorrow.

jokes.text: Something seems... Reba... she was anxious to get on the road. There was a big turquoise and silver fair in Santa Fe tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Larry sensing something wrong with show
jokes.punchline: Artie's ridiculous excuse for Reba's absence
jokes.explanation: Great Artie improvised excuse. The specific 'turquoise and silver fair' detail makes it funnier and more believable/unbelievable.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Something seems... Reba... she was anxious to get on the road. There was a big turquoise and silver fair in Santa Fe tomorrow.

jokes.text: Come on. You know this. He knew all the capitals of all the states. Backstage he was terrific. Come on. Starts with an 'I.' Ends with a 'y.'
jokes.setup: Roger freezing up on live TV during geography quiz
jokes.punchline: Hank's increasingly desperate coaching attempts
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy showing Hank's panic as his plan falls apart. The escalating desperation is painful and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Come on. You know this. He knew all the capitals of all the states. Backstage he was terrific. Come on. Starts with an 'I.' Ends with a 'y.'

jokes.text: Don't touch the boy.
jokes.setup: Hank getting increasingly physical in his coaching attempts
jokes.punchline: Larry's quiet but firm intervention
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan intervention. The economy and authority make this a standout moment of professional control.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Don't touch the boy.

jokes.text: Nice booking. The kid froze.
jokes.setup: Roger's complete failure on live television
jokes.punchline: Crew member's blunt assessment
jokes.explanation: Efficient post-mortem on the disaster. The understatement makes it funnier than overreaction would.

dialogue.line: Nice booking. The kid froze.

jokes.text: You are a grown man, you had no reason to attack him like that. He did great. It went great.
jokes.setup: Michelle confronting Hank about his TV behavior
jokes.punchline: Hank's delusional insistence it went well
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank delusion in the face of obvious disaster. His inability to process reality is character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Michelle
dialogue.line: You are a grown man, you had no reason to attack him like that. He did great. It went great.

jokes.text: You know, I've seen kids with Michael Jackson less nervous.
jokes.setup: Roger's nervous breakdown on TV
jokes.punchline: Artie's inappropriate Michael Jackson comparison
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and inappropriate Artie moment. The comparison is shocking but perfectly in character for his cynical worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You know, I've seen kids with Michael Jackson less nervous.

jokes.text: Just remember, this show is not about you getting laid. It's about Larry getting laid.
jokes.setup: Hank's romantic disaster affecting the show
jokes.punchline: Artie's crude but accurate assessment of show priorities
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie cynicism about show business hierarchies. Crude but accurate assessment of how things work.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Just remember, this show is not about you getting laid. It's about Larry getting laid.

jokes.text: Listen, I... I don't know how to tell you how sorry I am about that, because I just thought that she took the money... No, no, no. No, you misunderstood. Um, I came down here because... well, she did take the money.
jokes.setup: Larry apologizing for accusing Lori Loughlin
jokes.punchline: John's revelation that she actually did steal it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection and callback resolution. The audience expects an apology but gets validation of Larry's paranoia.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Listen, I... I don't know how to tell you how sorry I am about that, because I just thought that she took the money...

dialogue.speaker: John Stamos
dialogue.line: No, no, no. No, you misunderstood. Um, I came down here because... well, she did take the money.

jokes.text: No, you misunderstood. Um, I came down here because... well, she did take the money.
jokes.setup: Larry apologizing to John about Lori accusation
jokes.punchline: John confirming that Lori actually did steal the money
jokes.explanation: Perfect plot twist. Larry's paranoia proved right, John's awkward delivery of bad news about his friend.

dialogue.speaker: John Stamos
dialogue.line: No, you misunderstood. Um, I came down here because... well, she did take the money.

jokes.text: I know, it's weird. It's... she's, uh... she's had this problem for a while now really? Yeah, and she was embarrassed and she wanted me to bring the money down here, because she didn't, you know... you know, I mean, you could imagine how it...
jokes.setup: Confirming Lori's theft
jokes.punchline: John's awkward explanation of her ongoing kleptomania
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how awkward it is to explain away celebrity criminal behavior. John's stammering sells it.

dialogue.speaker: John Stamos
dialogue.line: I know, it's weird. It's... she's, uh... she's had this problem for a while now really? Yeah, and she was embarrassed and she wanted me to bring the money down here, because she didn't, you know... you know, I mean, you could imagine how it...

jokes.text: There was 250 in the wallet. There's 200 here. Is that a new earring?
jokes.setup: John returning most of the stolen money
jokes.punchline: Larry connecting John's new earring to the missing $50
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and escalation. Larry's paranoid attention to detail pays off, revealing another layer of theft.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: There was 250 in the wallet. There's 200 here. Is that a new earring?
